message.content: Adversaries may develop malware and its components to support their operations, including payloads, droppers, backdoors, packers, and C2 protocols. This capability allows them to maintain control over remote machines, evade defenses, and execute post-compromise activities. Malware development can be in-house or outsourced, with adversaries shaping requirements and maintaining exclusivity. Custom malware often requires additional infrastructure, such as web services for C2 communication. Notable groups like APT29, Lazarus Group, and Sandworm Team have developed unique malware tailored to their needs, including tools for ARP poisoning, credential dumping, and keystroke logging. Mitigation strategies include implementing robust endpoint protection, network segmentation, regular software updates, and monitoring for unusual activity to detect and respond to custom malware threats effectively.
